title: Ezekiel 29
subtitle: Oracle Against Egypt: Pharaoh the Sea Monster

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Oracle Against Egypt

sections.header: Verses 1-6a — Pharaoh the Great Monster in the Nile

heb.paragraph: Pharaoh is compared to the great tannin lurking in the Nile. The term evokes the primordial chaos-monster tradition found across ancient Near Eastern mythology (cf. Isa 51:9; Ps 74:13). Ezekiel transforms the mythological image into a concrete political metaphor: Egypt's king imagines himself lord of the Nile, but YHWH will hook him like a fish. The tannin motif connects to creation theology where God subdues chaos, implying that Egypt's power is merely another form of the primordial disorder that God sovereignly controls.

The yeʼor is the Egyptian loanword for the Nile, used exclusively in Hebrew for the great river of Egypt. Pharaoh's boast 'My Nile is my own; I made it for myself' (v. 3) represents the ultimate idolatrous claim—attributing creative power to a human ruler. In Egyptian royal theology, Pharaoh was indeed credited with controlling the Nile's inundation, so Ezekiel targets the very heart of Egyptian self-understanding.

refs.note: Isaiah also invokes the chaos-monster tradition, calling God to 'cut Rahab to pieces' and 'pierce the dragon.' Both prophets use mythological imagery to affirm YHWH's sovereignty over imperial powers.
Jeremiah warned that Egypt's army would retreat and Babylon would return to burn Jerusalem. Ezekiel's oracle against Egypt confirms Jeremiah's message from exile: Egypt cannot save Judah.
The dragon imagery is picked up in Revelation where the great dragon represents Satan's power behind earthly empires, extending Ezekiel's theological pattern of God subduing chaos-monsters.

notes.note: The date is January 587 BC. This is one of seven oracles against Egypt, the most against any single nation in Ezekiel. It was given while Nebuchadnezzar was besieging Jerusalem.

Pharaoh Hophra (Apries, 589-570 BC) is addressed. The 'great monster' (tannin) refers to the crocodile, Egypt's national symbol. His arrogant claim to have made the Nile reflects Egyptian theology that credited Pharaoh with the river's bounty.

God will hook the crocodile and drag it out, along with the fish clinging to its scales—the nations dependent on Egypt. They will be left unburied in the wilderness, a terrible fate in the ancient world.
Egypt proved to be a 'staff of reed' to Israel—appearing strong but splintering when leaned upon, wounding the hand that grasped it. This metaphor recalls 2 Kings 18:21.

Calvin observes that Pharaoh's pride in claiming the Nile as his own creation is the quintessential form of human arrogance—claiming for oneself what belongs to God alone. Every ruler who forgets that power is delegated falls into this same delusion.

Calvin notes the horror of being cast into the open field unburied: God strips away all the dignity that Egypt's rulers claimed, reducing the mighty to carrion. This demonstrates that no earthly grandeur can withstand divine judgment.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew tannin is variously rendered 'monster,' 'dragon,' or 'crocodile.' The Nile crocodile was a powerful symbol of Egyptian royal power, and the term carries mythological overtones of the chaos creature.
The hooks (Hebrew khakhim) in the jaws echo the practice of capturing crocodiles. The 'fish of your streams clinging to your scales' refers to Egypt's vassal states and allies.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies the tannin oracle as a carefully structured piece using the recognition formula ('then they will know that I am the LORD') as its climactic conclusion. The mythological imagery is domesticated into historical reality: Pharaoh is not a cosmic monster but a Nile crocodile who will be hauled out and left to rot. Block notes the irony: the creature that claims divine creative power is treated as mere game.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli connects the tannin motif to the broader ancient Near Eastern combat myth tradition but emphasizes Ezekiel's distinctive priestly transformation of the image. Unlike Isaiah's cosmic battle language, Ezekiel presents a fishing expedition—YHWH simply hooks the monster and discards it. The deflation of the mythological register serves Ezekiel's theological purpose: Egypt is not a worthy adversary for God.

hist.context: Dated to January 587 BC (the tenth year, tenth month, twelfth day), this oracle arrives during Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem. Judah's pro-Egyptian party hoped Pharaoh Hophra (Apries) would rescue them (cf. Jer 37:5-7). Ezekiel demolishes that hope by announcing Egypt's own judgment. The seven Egypt oracles (chs. 29-32) form the largest foreign-nation section in the book, reflecting Egypt's outsized role in Judah's political imagination.

heb.paragraph: The term shemamah describes total devastation—land so destroyed it provokes horror in those who see it. Applied to Egypt, this is an extraordinary reversal: the fertile Nile valley, symbol of agricultural abundance, will become like wilderness. The 40-year period of desolation parallels Israel's 40 years of wilderness wandering, suggesting a purgative exile for Egypt analogous to Israel's own formative experience.

refs.note: The Assyrian field commander mocked Hezekiah for trusting Egypt as a 'splintered reed of a staff, which pierces the hand of anyone who leans on it.' Ezekiel echoes this exact image.

Jeremiah also prophesied against Egypt, announcing that the LORD would punish Amon of Thebes and Pharaoh, but afterward Egypt would be inhabited again—a partial parallel to Ezekiel's restoration prediction.

notes.note: When Israel leaned on Egypt, Egypt splintered like a reed staff, dislocating shoulders and making knees buckle. This graphic imagery captures repeated historical betrayals: in 701 BC under Sennacherib, in 609 BC at Megiddo, and now during the current siege.
The desolation extends from Migdol (in the north, near the Mediterranean) to Syene (modern Aswan in the south)—the entire length of Egypt. No corner will be spared.
The 40-year desolation parallels Israel's wilderness wandering. Egypt's scattering among nations mirrors Israel's own exile, though Egypt's restoration will be to a diminished status.
Egypt will be restored as a 'lowly kingdom'—never again will it dominate nations. Israel will never again be tempted to rely on it, removing the perpetual snare of the Egyptian alliance.

Calvin argues that God targets Egypt precisely because Israel so persistently trusted in Egyptian power rather than in God. The destruction of Egypt is therefore a mercy to Israel: God removes the idol so that his people learn to rely on him alone.

Calvin emphasizes the theological purpose of Egypt's diminishment: Israel will no longer 'turn to them for help,' which was the great recurring sin. God often removes our false refuges to drive us back to himself.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The expression 'from Migdol to Syene' (Hebrew: mimigdol sevene) is a merism describing the full extent of Egypt from north to south. Syene is modern Aswan at the first cataract of the Nile.
Pathros refers to Upper (southern) Egypt. The description of Egypt as a 'lowly kingdom' (mamlakah shephelah) uses vocabulary that deliberately humiliates: shephelah implies being brought low.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block highlights the literary symmetry: Egypt's failure as a staff for Israel (v. 7) leads to its desolation (vv. 8-12), then a limited restoration (vv. 13-16). The 40-year period creates a deliberate parallel with Israel's wilderness experience, suggesting that Egypt too must undergo a purgative exile. Block notes that the promise of restoration—unique among Ezekiel's foreign-nation oracles—reflects Egypt's special theological status as Israel's 'origin story' adversary.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli observes that Egypt's restoration to 'lowly kingdom' status is remarkable within the oracles-against-the-nations genre, which typically concludes with total destruction. He suggests this reflects an older prophetic tradition that viewed Egypt as punishable but not annihilable, perhaps because Egypt's continued existence as a diminished power serves YHWH's purposes in the divine economy of nations.

hist.context: The staff-of-reed metaphor (v. 7) recalls Sennacherib's taunt in 2 Kings 18:21, where the Rabshakeh warned Hezekiah that relying on Egypt was like leaning on a broken reed. Egypt repeatedly promised military aid to Judah but failed to deliver. The 40-year desolation followed by a diminished return (vv. 13-16) finds no clear historical fulfillment, leading scholars to debate whether this is hyperbolic prophecy or refers to a partial fulfillment under Persian rule.

sections.header: Verses 17-21 — Egypt as Wages for Nebuchadnezzar

heb.paragraph: Sakhar denotes wages earned through labor. YHWH speaks as a divine employer, compensating Nebuchadnezzar for his 'service' against Tyre. The theological audacity is striking: a pagan emperor unwittingly serves as YHWH's hired laborer. This concept of God using foreign rulers as instruments of his will parallels Isaiah's calling Cyrus 'my anointed' (Isa 45:1) and demonstrates the absolute sovereignty of YHWH over all nations.

refs.note: God calls Cyrus 'my anointed' and says he will hand over nations to him. Both prophets share the radical theology that YHWH employs pagan rulers as instruments of his sovereign will.
Zechariah's prophecy that God 'has raised up a horn of salvation for us' echoes Ezekiel 29:21's promise of a horn sprouting for the house of Israel, connecting to messianic expectation.

notes.note: This is the latest dated oracle in Ezekiel—April 571 BC—placed here thematically rather than chronologically. It was given 16 years after the oracle in vv. 1-16.
Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Tyre lasted 13 years (585-573 BC) but yielded little plunder because the Tyrians evacuated their wealth by sea. God compensates his unwitting servant with Egypt's spoil.
The remarkable statement that Nebuchadnezzar 'worked for me' reveals divine sovereignty over pagan empires. God uses even those who do not know him to accomplish his purposes.
The 'horn' sprouting for Israel is a symbol of renewed strength and likely refers to the eventual restoration of the Davidic line, ultimately fulfilled in Christ.

Calvin marvels at God's sovereignty: Nebuchadnezzar labored for 13 years against Tyre without adequate pay, and God compensates him with another nation's wealth. Calvin draws the lesson that even unbelievers unwittingly serve God's purposes, and God is just enough to reward even pagan labor done in his service.

Calvin interprets the horn as a promise of restoration for Israel—God will cause hope to spring up even in the midst of judgment. He connects this to the Messiah, through whom Israel's true horn of salvation would arise.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The date formula corresponds to April 26, 571 BC. This is the latest dated oracle in the book, placed here for thematic rather than chronological reasons.

The Hebrew qeren ('horn') is a common metaphor for strength and power. The phrase 'opening of the mouth' may refer to Ezekiel's restored prophetic speech or to Israel's renewed ability to testify among the nations.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block calls this supplement the 'divine payroll' oracle. The late date (571 BC) means it was appended to the Egypt collection after the fact, demonstrating editorial awareness. Block emphasizes the theological innovation: YHWH operates as a cosmic employer who pays fair wages, even to a Babylonian king. The horn promise in v. 21 provides a rare positive note, linking the Egypt oracle cycle back to Israel's hope.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli notes that this is the only passage in Ezekiel where prophetic expectation is explicitly corrected by historical outcome. The Tyre siege did not produce the predicted plunder, so a new oracle redirects the divine compensation to Egypt. This remarkably honest editorial process reveals the living, adaptive nature of prophetic tradition.

hist.context: This is the latest dated oracle in Ezekiel: April 571 BC, some 16 years after the previous oracle in this chapter. Nebuchadnezzar's 13-year siege of Tyre (585-573 BC) ended inconclusively—he did not capture enough plunder to pay his army. God therefore 'gives' Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar as compensation. Historically, Nebuchadnezzar did campaign against Egypt in 568/567 BC. Verse 21 ('a horn will sprout for the house of Israel') is an enigmatic promise, possibly pointing to Jehoiachin's release or the future restoration.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates as YHWH commands even Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns. Judgment against Egypt is pronounced for its arrogance and unreliability. The horn promise introduces a faint note of mercy.

lit.note: The chapter combines two temporally distant oracles (587 and 571 BC) united by the theme of Egypt's judgment. The tannin imagery gives way to political-economic analysis, closing with an unexpected note of hope for Israel.

hebtext.note: Pharaoh is compared to the great tannin lurking in the Nile. The term evokes the primordial chaos-monster tradition found across ancient Near Eastern mythology (cf. Isa 51:9; Ps 74:13). Ezekiel transforms the mythological image into a concrete political metaphor: Egypt's king imagines himself lord of the Nile, but YHWH will hook him like a fish. The tannin motif connects to creation theology where God subdues chaos, implying that Egypt's power is merely another form of the primordial disorder that God sovereignly controls.

The yeʼor is the Egyptian loanword for the Nile, used exclusively in Hebrew for the great river of Egypt. Pharaoh's boast 'My Nile is my own; I made it for myself' (v. 3) represents the ultimate idolatrous claim—attributing creative power to a human ruler. In Egyptian royal theology, Pharaoh was indeed credited with controlling the Nile's inundation, so Ezekiel targets the very heart of Egyptian self-understanding.

The term shemamah describes total devastation—land so destroyed it provokes horror in those who see it. Applied to Egypt, this is an extraordinary reversal: the fertile Nile valley, symbol of agricultural abundance, will become like wilderness. The 40-year period of desolation parallels Israel's 40 years of wilderness wandering, suggesting a purgative exile for Egypt analogous to Israel's own formative experience.

Sakhar denotes wages earned through labor. YHWH speaks as a divine employer, compensating Nebuchadnezzar for his 'service' against Tyre. The theological audacity is striking: a pagan emperor unwittingly serves as YHWH's hired laborer. This concept of God using foreign rulers as instruments of his will parallels Isaiah's calling Cyrus 'my anointed' (Isa 45:1) and demonstrates the absolute sovereignty of YHWH over all nations.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oracle Against Egypt: Pharaoh the Sea Monster

translations.text: But I will put hooks in your jaws and make the fish of your streams stick to your scales. I will pull you out from among your streams, with all the fish sticking to your scales.

I will put hooks in your jaws, and make the fish of your streams stick to your scales; and I will draw you up out of the midst of your streams, with all the fish of your streams that stick to your scales.

I will leave you in the desert, you and all the fish of your streams. You will fall on the open field and not be gathered or picked up. I will give you as food to the beasts of the earth and the birds of the sky.

And I will cast you out into the wilderness, you and all the fish of your streams; you shall fall on the open field, and not be brought together or gathered. To the beasts of the earth and to the birds of the heavens I give you as food.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The date is January 587 BC. This is one of seven oracles against Egypt, the most against any single nation in Ezekiel. It was given while Nebuchadnezzar was besieging Jerusalem."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin observes that Pharaoh's pride in claiming the Nile as his own creation is the quintessential form of human arrogance—claiming for oneself what belongs to God alone. Every ruler who forgets that…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The Hebrew tannin is variously rendered 'monster,' 'dragon,' or 'crocodile.' The Nile crocodile was a powerful symbol of Egyptian royal power, and the term carries mythological overtones of the chaos …"

debate: The date formula corresponds to April 26, 571 BC. This is the latest dated oracle in the book, placed here for thematic rather than chronological reas…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Oracle Against Egypt within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Isaiah also invokes the chaos-monster tradition, calling God to 'cut Rahab to pieces' and 'pierce the dragon.' Both prophets use mythological imagery to affirm YHWH's sovereignty over imperial powers.
Jeremiah warned that Egypt's army would retreat and Babylon would return to burn Jerusalem. Ezekiel's oracle against Egypt confirms Jeremiah's message from exile: Egypt cannot save Judah.
The dragon imagery is picked up in Revelation where the great dragon represents Satan's power behind earthly empires, extending Ezekiel's theological pattern of God subduing chaos-monsters.
The Assyrian field commander mocked Hezekiah for trusting Egypt as a 'splintered reed of a staff, which pierces the hand of anyone who leans on it.' Ezekiel echoes this exact image.

Jeremiah also prophesied against Egypt, announcing that the LORD would punish Amon of Thebes and Pharaoh, but afterward Egypt would be inhabited again—a partial parallel to Ezekiel's restoration prediction.
God calls Cyrus 'my anointed' and says he will hand over nations to him. Both prophets share the radical theology that YHWH employs pagan rulers as instruments of his sovereign will.

coaching.tip: Pharaoh is depicted as a 'great monster' in the Nile who says 'The Nile belongs to me; I made it for myself' (v. 3). The claim of self-creation echoes the prince of Tyre's self-deification in chapter 28. Ezekiel sees the same sin in different empires: power attributing its success to itself rather than to God. The oracles against the nations diagnose a universal disease; only the symptoms vary.

God gives Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar as 'wages' for his service against Tyre (v. 19) — because the Tyre campaign didn't pay. God compensates a pagan king for services rendered. The theology is bracingly practical: God uses empires and pays them in conquered territory. Sovereignty in Ezekiel isn't abstract — it operates through the logic of imperial economics, subverting it from within.
